facts.text: The America's Cup is the oldest international sporting trophy, predating the modern Olympics by 45 years.
Port refers to the left side of a boat when facing the bow, and starboard refers to the right side.
The Vendée Globe is a solo, non-stop, unassisted around-the-world yacht race held every four years.
Sir Robin Knox-Johnston became the first person to sail solo non-stop around the world in 1969.
A sextant measures the angle between a celestial body and the horizon to determine position at sea.
The Beaufort scale classifies wind speed from 0 (calm) to 12 (hurricane force).
Polynesian wayfinders navigated thousands of miles across the Pacific using stars, waves, and bird flight patterns.
The Laser is one of the most popular single-handed sailing dinghies in the world, with over 200,000 built.
Tacking is the maneuver of turning the bow of the boat through the wind to change direction.
The trade winds are consistent easterly winds found in the tropics, historically crucial for sailing commerce.
Dame Ellen MacArthur set the world record for the fastest solo circumnavigation in 2005, completing it in 71 days.
A catamaran has two hulls, providing greater stability and speed compared to a monohull sailboat.
The Golden Globe Race was first held in 1968 as the first solo non-stop around-the-world yacht race.
Joshua Slocum was the first person to sail single-handedly around the world, completing his voyage in 1898.
Dead reckoning is a method of navigation using speed, time, and direction to estimate position without celestial observation.
The 49er is a high-performance two-person skiff that has been an Olympic sailing class since the 2000 Sydney Games.
J-Class yachts were the grand racing yachts that competed for the America's Cup in the 1930s.
The doldrums, or Intertropical Convergence Zone, is a belt of calm winds near the equator dreaded by sailors.
SailGP is a global sailing league featuring identical foiling F50 catamarans racing in iconic harbors.
A spinnaker is a large, balloon-shaped sail used for sailing downwind.
The Rolex Sydney Hobart Yacht Race starts every year on Boxing Day from Sydney Harbour.
Ferdinand Magellan's expedition completed the first circumnavigation of the Earth between 1519 and 1522.
A trimaran has three hulls and can achieve very high speeds due to its wide beam and light weight.
The Nacra 17 is a mixed-gender foiling catamaran class at the Olympic Games.
Clipper ships were the fastest sailing vessels of the 19th century, designed for speed in the tea and wool trades.
Windward means the direction from which the wind is blowing, while leeward is the direction the wind is blowing toward.
The Caribbean is one of the most popular cruising destinations in the world, offering steady trade winds and sheltered anchorages.
The Volvo Ocean Race (now The Ocean Race) is a crewed around-the-world race held approximately every three years.
A keel is a weighted fin on the bottom of a sailboat that prevents it from capsizing and sliding sideways.
Bernard Moitessier famously abandoned the 1968 Golden Globe Race while in the lead, choosing to continue sailing rather than finish.
The first America's Cup race was held on August 22, 1851, around the Isle of Wight.
GPS (Global Positioning System) revolutionized marine navigation when it became available to civilians in the 1980s.
A jibe (or gybe) is turning the stern of the boat through the wind, the opposite of tacking.
The Cutty Sark, launched in 1869, is one of the last and most famous clipper ships ever built.
Viking longships used both sails and oars and could navigate open ocean as well as shallow rivers.
Bareboat chartering allows sailors to rent a boat without a crew and navigate it themselves.
The Mediterranean is home to some of the world's best sailing grounds, including the Greek islands, Croatia, and Turkey.
A schooner is a sailing vessel with two or more masts, with the foremast being shorter than the mainmast.
The points of sail describe a boat's course relative to the wind: close-hauled, beam reach, broad reach, and running.
Team New Zealand has won the America's Cup five times, most recently defending it in Barcelona in 2024.
The Fastnet Race is a classic offshore yacht race from Cowes to the Fastnet Rock and back to Plymouth.
A halyard is a rope used to hoist sails, flags, or yards on a sailing vessel.
Jessica Watson became the youngest person to sail solo, non-stop, and unassisted around the world at age 16 in 2010.
The South Pacific offers pristine cruising grounds including Fiji, Tonga, and French Polynesia.
The Optimist dinghy is the most popular youth sailing class in the world, sailed in over 120 countries.
A compass rose on a nautical chart shows the directions of true north and magnetic north.
The Bermuda Triangle has long been associated with mysterious disappearances of ships and aircraft.
Running rigging refers to the ropes and lines used to control sails, while standing rigging supports the mast.
The 470 is a two-person dinghy that has been an Olympic sailing class since the 1976 Montreal Games.
Christopher Columbus set sail from Palos de la Frontera, Spain, on August 3, 1492.
A keelboat is a sailboat with a fixed keel, as opposed to a dinghy which has a retractable centerboard.
Weather routing uses meteorological data to plan the fastest or safest course for a sailing passage.
The AC75 foiling monohull was introduced for the 36th America's Cup in Auckland in 2021.
Sir Francis Chichester completed the first solo circumnavigation with only one stop in 1967.
A tell-tale is a small piece of yarn or ribbon attached to the sail to indicate airflow direction.
The Whitbread Round the World Race, first held in 1973, was the precursor to the Volvo Ocean Race.
Liveaboard sailors live full-time on their boats, often cruising from port to port.
The bow is the front of the boat and the stern is the back.
Laura Dekker became the youngest person to complete a solo circumnavigation at age 16 in 2012.
A nautical mile is approximately 1.15 statute miles or 1.852 kilometers.
The Finn class was the single-handed Olympic dinghy from 1952 to 2020, replaced by the foiling ILCA 7.
Fleet racing involves multiple boats racing around a course, with positions determining points.
The Strait of Magellan connects the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans at the southern tip of South America.
A boom is the horizontal spar that extends from the mast and holds the bottom edge of the mainsail.
Match racing pits two boats against each other in one-on-one competition, as used in the America's Cup.
The Vendée Globe starts and finishes at Les Sables-d'Olonne in France.
A knot is a unit of speed equal to one nautical mile per hour, approximately 1.15 mph.
The Great Barrier Reef in Australia is a spectacular but challenging cruising destination.
Reefing is the process of reducing the area of a sail to manage heavy winds.
The Star class was one of the longest-running Olympic sailing classes, used from 1932 to 2012.
Oracle Team USA staged the greatest comeback in America's Cup history in 2013, winning 8 straight races after trailing 1-8.
Celestial navigation uses the positions of the sun, moon, stars, and planets to determine a vessel's location.
The Transat Jacques Vabre is a transatlantic race from Le Havre, France, to Salvador, Brazil.
A genoa is a large jib sail that overlaps the mast, providing extra power in lighter winds.
The Beaufort scale was devised by Sir Francis Beaufort of the Royal Navy in 1805.
A drogue is a device towed behind a boat to slow it down in heavy weather.
The British Virgin Islands are considered one of the easiest and most beautiful bareboat charter destinations.
A mainsheet is the line used to control the angle of the mainsail relative to the wind.
The Route du Rhum is a solo transatlantic race from Saint-Malo, France, to Pointe-à-Pitre, Guadeloupe.
Ben Ainslie is the most successful Olympic sailor in history, with four gold medals and one silver.
A sea anchor is a parachute-like device deployed in the water to keep a boat's bow into the wind.
The age of sail spanned roughly from the 16th to the mid-19th century, when wind was the primary power for ships.
A shackle is a U-shaped piece of metal used to connect parts of rigging or anchor chain.
The Hobie Cat 16 is one of the most popular recreational catamarans ever produced.
Apparent wind is the wind as felt on a moving boat, a combination of true wind and the boat's own speed.
The Clipper Round the World Race is open to amateur sailors, with professional skippers leading each leg.
The first Olympic sailing event took place at the 1900 Paris Games.
A winch is a mechanical device on a sailboat used to tighten lines and sheets under load.
Storm sails are small, heavily reinforced sails designed for use in severe weather conditions.
The Panama Canal opened in 1914, eliminating the need to sail around Cape Horn between the Atlantic and Pacific.
A bowsprit is a spar extending forward from the bow of a sailing vessel.
The Mini Transat is a solo transatlantic race for boats just 6.5 meters (21 feet) long.
Close-hauled is the point of sail closest to the wind, typically about 30-45 degrees from the wind direction.
The Whitsunday Islands in Australia offer sheltered sailing among 74 tropical islands.
A boom vang is a line or mechanical device that prevents the boom from rising when sailing downwind.
The Suez Canal opened in 1869, dramatically shortening the sailing route between Europe and Asia.
EPIRB (Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon) transmits distress signals to search and rescue satellites.
Alinghi from Switzerland won the America's Cup in 2003, becoming the first European winner in 152 years.
The Solent, between the Isle of Wight and mainland England, is one of the busiest sailing waters in the world.
A traveler is a track mounted across the boat that allows the mainsheet attachment point to slide side to side.
Magellan's fleet departed Spain on September 20, 1519, with five ships and about 270 men.
A radar reflector increases a small boat's visibility on other vessels' radar screens.
The Sydney to Hobart race covers 628 nautical miles through some of the most challenging waters in the world.
Heaving to is a technique where the sails and rudder are set to counteract each other, effectively pausing the boat.
Paul Elvstrøm of Denmark won four consecutive Olympic gold medals in sailing from 1948 to 1960.
A bilge pump removes water that has entered the hull of a boat.
The Cape of Good Hope at the southern tip of Africa was a notorious hazard for sailing ships.
Sail trim is the adjustment of sails to optimize their shape and angle for current wind conditions.
The first Vendée Globe race started on November 26, 1989.
A batten is a thin strip inserted into a pocket in the sail to help maintain its shape.
The 505 is a high-performance two-person dinghy known for its speed and demanding sailing technique.
Francis Joyon holds the solo around-the-world sailing record at 40 days, 23 hours, and 30 minutes.
Antifouling paint is applied to the bottom of boats to prevent the growth of marine organisms.
The Strait of Hormuz is one of the most strategically important waterways for maritime traffic.
A preventer is a line rigged to keep the boom from swinging unexpectedly during a jibe.
The Sunfish is one of the most popular recreational sailboats ever made, with over 300,000 produced.
The International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs) govern right-of-way on the water.
The English Channel is one of the busiest shipping lanes in the world and a challenging body of water to sail.
A cleat is a fitting on a boat used to secure a line without having to tie a knot.
The Transpac race runs from Los Angeles to Honolulu, covering approximately 2,225 nautical miles.
Sail numbers identify racing boats and are displayed on the mainsail during competitions.
The Roaring Forties are strong westerly winds found between 40 and 50 degrees south latitude.
An anchor windlass is a mechanical device used to raise and lower the anchor.
The Annapolis to Bermuda race is a 753-nautical-mile ocean race from Maryland to Bermuda.
A shroud is a piece of standing rigging that runs from the mast to the side of the boat.
Columbus reached the Bahamas on October 12, 1492, after 36 days at sea.
Hydrofoils lift the hull of a boat out of the water, dramatically reducing drag and increasing speed.
The Sardinia Cup is one of the premier Mediterranean offshore sailing events.
A fairlead is a fitting that guides a line and prevents chafing.
The IDEC Sport trimaran set the fastest circumnavigation record in 2017, completing it in 40 days.
Lee helm and weather helm describe the tendency of a boat to turn toward or away from the wind.
The Bay of Islands in New Zealand is a world-class cruising destination with over 140 subtropical islands.
A turnbuckle is a device used to adjust the tension of standing rigging.
The first solo non-stop circumnavigation was completed by Robin Knox-Johnston aboard Suhaili in 1969.
Heeling is when a sailboat leans to one side due to wind pressure on the sails.
The Antigua Sailing Week is one of the Caribbean's premier annual sailing regattas.
A chart plotter combines GPS data with electronic nautical charts for modern marine navigation.
The Strait of Gibraltar connects the Atlantic Ocean to the Mediterranean Sea.
A gooseneck is the fitting that connects the boom to the mast, allowing it to pivot.
The Newport Bermuda Race has been held biennially since 1906, covering 635 nautical miles.
Sailing was part of every Summer Olympics from 1900 to the present, except for the 1904 Games.
The Furious Fifties are winds between 50 and 60 degrees south latitude, even stronger than the Roaring Forties.
A rudder is the underwater blade at the stern used to steer the boat.
The Sunsail fleet operates bareboat charters in over 30 destinations worldwide.
Displacement refers to the weight of water displaced by a boat's hull, equal to the boat's weight.
The original America's Cup trophy was won by the schooner America in a race around the Isle of Wight in 1851.
A spreader is a horizontal strut on the mast that pushes the shrouds outward for better support.
The Admiral's Cup was a premier international offshore sailing competition held in the Solent from 1957 to 2003.
An asymmetric spinnaker is flown from a bowsprit and is easier to handle than a traditional symmetric spinnaker.
The Chesapeake Bay is the largest estuary in the United States and a popular sailing area.
A mooring is a permanent anchoring point where boats can tie up without using their own anchor.
The Flying Dutchman is both a legendary ghost ship and a former Olympic sailing class.
Latitude and longitude form the coordinate system used to specify positions on Earth's surface for navigation.
The Cape Horn is the southernmost headland of South America and one of the most dangerous passages in sailing.
A daggerboard is a retractable keel that slides vertically through a slot in the hull.
The Marion Bermuda Race is a 645-nautical-mile race that emphasizes celestial navigation.
Wingsails are rigid, wing-shaped sails that generate more power than traditional soft sails.
The Giraglia Rolex Cup is a prestigious offshore race in the Mediterranean, starting from Saint-Tropez.
A tiller is a lever connected to the rudder, used to steer smaller sailboats.
Australia II won the America's Cup in 1983, ending the New York Yacht Club's 132-year winning streak.
The Sailing World Championships bring together all Olympic and non-Olympic classes in a single event.
A jib is the triangular sail set forward of the mast.
The Strait of Malacca between the Malay Peninsula and Sumatra is one of the most important shipping routes.
Fenders are cushions hung over the side of a boat to protect it when docking.
The BVI Spring Regatta is a major Caribbean racing event held annually in the British Virgin Islands.
A masthead is the top of the mast, where wind instruments and navigation lights are typically mounted.
The Hauraki Gulf near Auckland, New Zealand, is known as the City of Sails for its sailing culture.
Tidal currents can significantly affect boat speed and must be factored into navigation planning.
The Etchells is a popular one-design keelboat class used worldwide for competitive racing.
A block is a pulley used on a sailboat to redirect lines and create mechanical advantage.
Sir Francis Drake completed the second circumnavigation of the globe between 1577 and 1580.
Sail area to displacement ratio is a key performance indicator for comparing sailboat speed potential.
The America's Cup was held in San Francisco in 2013, marking the first time it featured foiling catamarans.
A sea breeze develops during the day as warm air over land rises and cooler air from the sea flows in.
The 420 dinghy is a popular youth racing class that feeds into Olympic pathways.
A spring line is a dock line that prevents a boat from moving forward or backward along the dock.
The Round the Island Race around the Isle of Wight is the largest yacht race in the world by number of entries.
VHF radio Channel 16 is the international distress and calling frequency for maritime communications.
The ketch rig has two masts with the mizzen mast shorter and located forward of the rudder post.
A chartroom or nav station is the area on a boat dedicated to navigation instruments and charts.
The Pacific Cup race runs from San Francisco to Kaneohe Bay, Hawaii.
Tonnage in sailing refers to the volume of a vessel's enclosed spaces, not its weight.
The RSS Discovery was the last traditional wooden three-masted ship built in Britain, launched in 1901.
Leech, luff, and foot are the three edges of a triangular sail.
SailGP boats can reach speeds exceeding 50 knots (about 57 mph or 93 km/h).
A watermaker or desalinator converts seawater to fresh water using reverse osmosis, essential for long ocean passages.
The 12 Metre class was used for America's Cup racing from 1958 to 1987.
A backstay is a piece of standing rigging running from the top of the mast to the stern.
The Mackinac Race on the Great Lakes is the longest freshwater sailing race in the world.
Magnetic declination is the angle between true north and magnetic north, varying by location and over time.
The Tall Ships Races are annual sailing events for large sail training vessels, organized since 1956.
A cockpit is the recessed area in the deck where the helmsman and crew sit while sailing.
The Gulf Stream is a powerful ocean current that affects sailing routes in the North Atlantic.
A topping lift is a line that supports the boom when the mainsail is lowered.
The Mini 6.50 class features solo races across the Atlantic in boats only 6.5 meters long.
Sail twist describes the change in angle of attack from the bottom to the top of a sail.
The first women's Olympic sailing event was held at the 1988 Seoul Games.
A sea state describes the condition of the ocean surface based on wave height and period.
The Dalmatian Coast of Croatia is one of Europe's most beautiful and popular sailing destinations.
A cunningham is a control line used to adjust the tension on the luff of a sail.
Ellen MacArthur retired from competitive sailing in 2010 to focus on the Ellen MacArthur Foundation for circular economy.
Laminar flow over a sail creates lift similar to an airplane wing, propelling the boat forward.
The Figaro solo race in France is considered one of the best training grounds for offshore racing talent.
A companionway is the stairway or ladder leading from the deck to the cabin below.
The Amalfi Coast in Italy offers dramatic coastal sailing with stunning cliff-side villages.
A vang (or kicking strap) controls the vertical angle of the boom.
The OSTAR (Observer Single-Handed Trans-Atlantic Race) was first held in 1960.
A reef knot (or square knot) is one of the most basic and essential sailing knots.
The Volvo Ocean Race 2017-18 was the first edition to have all-identical one-design boats.
A depth sounder uses sonar to measure the water depth beneath a boat's hull.
The Gulf of Thailand offers warm-water sailing with islands like Koh Samui and Phuket.
A gennaker is a hybrid between a genoa and a spinnaker, used for reaching in moderate winds.
The oldest known boat, the Pesse canoe, dates to approximately 8000 BCE.
Canting keels can be tilted to windward to reduce heeling and increase speed on modern racing yachts.
A jackline is a line run along the deck to which crew members clip their safety harnesses.
The Ionian Islands in Greece offer gentle winds and calm seas, perfect for beginner sailors.
A whisker pole holds out the jib when sailing downwind to prevent it from collapsing.
The Fastnet Race disaster of 1979 killed 15 sailors and led to major reforms in offshore safety.
A bollard is a strong post on a dock or ship used for securing mooring lines.
The IRC (International Rating Certificate) handicap system allows different boats to race fairly against each other.
The bowline knot creates a fixed loop at the end of a rope and is considered the most useful knot in sailing.
A fathom is a unit of depth measurement equal to 6 feet or 1.83 meters.
The Turkish Riviera (Turquoise Coast) offers sheltered bays and ancient ruins for cruising sailors.
A head is the nautical term for a boat's toilet.
The Spanish Armada sailed from Lisbon in May 1588 with 130 ships to invade England.
A lazy jack is a set of lines running from the mast to the boom that guide the mainsail when lowering.
The IMOCA 60 is the class of boat used in the Vendée Globe, measuring 60 feet (18.28 meters).
Hydrofoiling has become the biggest revolution in competitive sailing in the 21st century.
A life raft is a mandatory piece of safety equipment for offshore sailing.
The San Juan Islands in Washington State are a premier Pacific Northwest sailing destination.
A starboard tack means the wind is coming over the starboard (right) side of the boat.
The Kon-Tiki expedition in 1947 proved that ancient peoples could have sailed across the Pacific on balsa rafts.
A chain plate is a metal fitting that attaches the shrouds to the hull of a boat.
The Moth class features a single-person foiling dinghy that can fly above the water at high speeds.
Apparent wind angle and speed are typically displayed on instruments at the top of the mast.
The Aegean Sea between Greece and Turkey has been a sailing route for over 4,000 years.
A samson post is a strong post on the foredeck used for securing anchor rode or tow lines.
The Bermuda rig (or Marconi rig) with a triangular mainsail is the most common sail plan on modern boats.
A bimini top is a canvas canopy over the cockpit that provides shade from the sun.
Sailing vessels have right of way over power boats under most circumstances in the COLREGs.
A padeye is a fixed deck fitting used as an attachment point for blocks and lines.
The Canary Islands are a popular jumping-off point for transatlantic sailing passages.
Sailing by the lee means sailing downwind with the wind coming slightly over the same side as the boom.
The Battle of Trafalgar was fought on October 21, 1805, giving Lord Nelson a posthumous victory.
A pelican hook is a quick-release hook used on lifelines and life rafts.
The Class 40 is a 40-foot ocean racing class designed for short-handed sailing.
Chinese junk rigs feature battened sails that can be reefed simply by lowering them partway.
A leeboard is a foil mounted on the side of a flat-bottomed boat to prevent leeway.
Tahiti and the Society Islands in French Polynesia are a dream destination for long-distance cruising sailors.
A sheet is a rope used to control the angle of a sail; it does not refer to the sail itself.
The Laser was designed by Bruce Kirby in 1969 and became an Olympic class in 1996.
A monohull sailboat with a deep keel is inherently self-righting after a knockdown.
The Strait of Messina between Sicily and mainland Italy is known for strong and unpredictable currents.
A rode is the combination of chain and rope that connects an anchor to the boat.
The Battle of Lepanto in 1571 was the last major naval engagement fought primarily between oar-powered galleys.
An autopilot can steer a sailboat on a set compass heading or wind angle, freeing the crew for other tasks.
The biennial Chicago to Mackinac Race has been held since 1898 on Lake Michigan.
A gaff rig has a four-sided mainsail supported by a spar (gaff) at the top.
Marine VHF radios operate on frequencies between 156.0 and 163.275 MHz.
The Great Lakes offer extensive freshwater sailing, with Lake Michigan being particularly popular.
A burgee is a small triangular flag flown from the masthead to indicate yacht club membership.
The Mayflower departed Plymouth, England, on September 16, 1620, carrying 102 passengers to the New World.
A pinnacle is the stand or housing for a ship's compass.
The Barcelona World Race is a two-handed non-stop round-the-world race.
The Wally class features luxury high-performance yachts designed for both racing and cruising.
A catboat has a single mast set far forward and one large sail, popular on Cape Cod.
The Seychelles in the Indian Ocean offer pristine anchorages among granite islands.
A clew is the lower aft corner of a sail where the sheet is attached.
Air draft is the height of a boat's mast above the waterline, important for clearing bridges.
A yawl rig has two masts with the smaller mizzen mast located aft of the rudder post.
Peter Blake won the Whitbread Round the World Race twice and the Jules Verne Trophy.
A bosun's chair is a seat used to hoist a person up the mast for repairs or inspections.
The Marquesas Islands in the South Pacific are often the first landfall for sailors coming from the Americas.
Chafe gear protects lines and sails from wearing through due to friction.
The first transatlantic yacht race took place in December 1866 between three schooners.
A yuloh is a Chinese sculling oar that can propel a boat efficiently without an engine.
The Offshore Racing Congress (ORC) develops rating systems for fair handicap racing.
The Gulf of Maine offers challenging sailing with strong tides and fog.
A lazarette is a storage compartment in the stern of a boat.
The Azores are a mid-Atlantic staging point for sailors crossing between Europe and the Americas.
A boom preventer stops the boom from accidentally jibing across in heavy following seas.
The Tea Clipper races between China and England in the 1860s were the Formula 1 of their era.
A downhaul is a line used to pull a sail or spar downward.
New Zealand is known as the cradle of modern foiling technology in competitive sailing.
A portlight is a small window in the hull or cabin of a boat.
The Galapagos Islands are a waypoint for Pacific cruising sailors heading from the Americas toward Polynesia.
A sea cock is a valve in the hull that allows or prevents water from entering the boat.
HMS Victory, Nelson's flagship at Trafalgar, is the oldest commissioned warship still in existence.
A bobstay is a wire or chain running from the bowsprit to the waterline to keep it from lifting.
The Jules Verne Trophy is awarded for the fastest circumnavigation by any crew in any yacht.
A fairwind or following wind blows from behind the boat, making for easy downwind sailing.
The Red Sea offers spectacular sailing with warm waters, coral reefs, and steady winds.
A Samoan cleat (or horn cleat) is one of the simplest and most common types of cleats on boats.
The schooner Bluenose from Nova Scotia was the fastest fishing schooner in the North Atlantic in the 1920s and 1930s.
A tabernacle is a hinge at the base of a mast allowing it to be lowered for passing under bridges.
Joshua Slocum's boat, the Spray, was a 36-foot oyster sloop he rebuilt himself for his solo circumnavigation.
A headfoil is an extrusion fitted to the forestay that holds the luff of a roller-furling jib.
The Torres Strait between Australia and Papua New Guinea is a challenging passage with reefs and strong currents.
A Danforth anchor features flat flukes that dig into sandy or muddy bottoms.
The Phoenicians were among the earliest known seafaring traders, sailing the Mediterranean around 1500 BCE.
A kedge is a light anchor used for temporarily anchoring or hauling a boat off a sandbar.
The Vendée Globe is nicknamed 'the Everest of the seas' due to its extreme difficulty.
A pelorus is a sighting device on a boat's compass used for taking bearings.
The Intracoastal Waterway runs along the U.S. Atlantic and Gulf coasts, offering protected sailing.
A roller furling system allows a jib or genoa to be rolled up around the forestay for easy handling.
The clipper ship Thermopylae once sailed from London to Melbourne in just 60 days in 1868.
A dinghy is a small open boat, often used as a tender for larger vessels or for recreational sailing.
Greta Thunberg sailed across the Atlantic in a racing yacht in 2019 to avoid flying.
The isobars on a weather map show lines of equal atmospheric pressure.
The Aeolian Islands off Sicily offer volcanic landscapes and excellent Mediterranean sailing.
A leeward mark is a racing buoy located on the downwind side of the course.
Captain James Cook made three voyages of exploration to the Pacific between 1768 and 1779.
A plow anchor (CQR) is designed to reset itself if the boat swings with wind or current changes.
The Sydney to Hobart race in 1998 was the deadliest edition, with six sailors losing their lives.
A battcar is a fitting that connects the batten to a track on the mast.
A fiddle is a raised edge on a table or shelf aboard a boat that prevents items from sliding off.
The Bay of Fundy between Nova Scotia and New Brunswick has the highest tides in the world.
A breasthook is a strengthening piece at the bow of a boat where the hull sides meet.
The America's Cup Challenger Series (Prada Cup) determines which team earns the right to challenge the defender.
A staysail is a triangular sail set on a stay running from the mast to the deck.
Captain Joshua Slocum departed Boston on April 24, 1895, to begin his historic solo circumnavigation.
The Vendée Globe has never been won by a woman, though several female sailors have competed.
A centerboard pivots down through a slot in the hull to reduce leeway, similar to a daggerboard.
The Cyclades islands in Greece offer reliable meltemi winds during summer sailing season.
A cringle is a reinforced ring or grommet in a sail, used for attaching lines.
VHF radio is the primary means of short-range communication between vessels at sea.
A catenary is the curve formed by an anchor chain hanging between the boat and the seabed.
The Transat AG2R La Mondiale is a two-handed transatlantic race run in identical Figaro 3 boats.
The fastest sailing vessel ever recorded was the Vestas Sailrocket 2, reaching 65.45 knots in 2012.
A hand bearing compass is a portable compass held in the hand for taking bearings on landmarks.
The Maldives offer flat-water sailing among coral atolls in the Indian Ocean.
A painter is a short rope at the bow of a dinghy used for tying it up.
The square rig with sails set perpendicular to the hull was the standard for large sailing ships for centuries.
A toe rail is a low rail around the outer edge of the deck.
The America's Cup was held in Bermuda for the first time in 2017.
The International Moth class has played a pioneering role in the development of foiling dinghy technology.
A jackstay is a rod or wire along a yard or bowsprit to which sails are fastened.
Puget Sound in Washington State offers sheltered sailing with views of the Cascade and Olympic mountains.
A windsock or wind indicator at a marina shows wind direction at the surface level.
The Phoenician sailor Hanno the Navigator explored the west coast of Africa around 500 BCE.
A sprit is a spar that extends diagonally across a four-sided sail.
The first Whitbread Round the World Race started on September 8, 1973.
The foiling AC75 boats used in the 37th America's Cup can sail at nearly twice the wind speed.
The ILCA (International Laser Class Association) governs the Laser/ILCA dinghy class worldwide.
The Adriatic Sea along Italy and Croatia is a popular charter sailing destination.
A broach is an uncontrolled turn caused by too much sail or wave action, often leading to a knockdown.
Radar is used at sea for collision avoidance and coastal navigation in poor visibility.
A galley is the kitchen area on a boat.
Pip Hare became the first British woman to finish the Vendée Globe in 2021.
AIS (Automatic Identification System) broadcasts a vessel's identity, position, course, and speed to nearby ships.
The Tonga Islands in the South Pacific are a favorite stop for cruising sailors crossing the Pacific.
A tang is a metal fitting at the masthead or on the mast to which stays and shrouds are attached.
The Battle of the Nile in 1798 saw Nelson destroy the French fleet in Aboukir Bay, Egypt.
A thimble is a metal or plastic fitting that protects the inside of a rope eye or loop.
Yachting became an organized sport in the 17th century when King Charles II of England raced on the Thames.
The J/70 is a popular one-design keelboat for both fleet racing and club sailing.
A flemish coil is a flat spiral of rope used on deck for a tidy and decorative appearance.
The Marquesas to Tuamotus passage in French Polynesia is a classic trade wind sail.
A running backstay is an adjustable stay that supports the mast when sailing off the wind.
The first known illustration of a boat under sail is on a painted disc from approximately 5500 BCE in Kuwait.
A leech line runs through the trailing edge of a sail to prevent fluttering.
The Transat 6.50 solo race from France to Brazil covers approximately 4,000 miles in a 21-foot boat.
Draft is the depth of water needed to float a boat; a shallow draft allows sailing in thin water.
The Cape Verde Islands are a common waypoint for sailors crossing the Atlantic from Europe.
A gollywobbler is a large, full sail set between the masts of a schooner in light airs.
The International Sailing Federation (now World Sailing) was founded in 1907 in Paris.
A fairway buoy marks the center of a navigable channel.
The Singlehanded Transpac race from San Francisco to Hanalei Bay, Kauai, covers 2,120 nautical miles.
Le Maître CoQ and PRB are among the famous IMOCA 60 boats that have competed in the Vendée Globe.
A barber hauler is a line used to adjust the sheeting angle of a jib or genoa.
The Panama Canal allows sailors to transit between the Atlantic and Pacific in about 8-10 hours.
A running fix uses two bearings on the same object taken at different times to determine position.
HMS Bounty's mutiny occurred on April 28, 1789, in the South Pacific.
A skeg is a fixed fin ahead of the rudder that protects it and improves tracking.
The America's Cup in 2024 was held in Barcelona, Spain, with racing on the Mediterranean.
A sea cock or through-hull fitting allows water to be pumped in or drained from a boat.
The Coral Sea between Australia and the Solomon Islands is a challenging blue-water sailing area.
A bridle is a two-legged line spread between two points, used for anchoring or towing.
Captain Bligh sailed 3,618 nautical miles in an open launch after the Bounty mutiny, one of maritime history's greatest feats of seamanship.
A reaching strut extends a shroud outward for better support when the mast is loaded on a reach.
The word 'posh' is said to derive from 'Port Out, Starboard Home,' referring to the shaded cabins on ships traveling to and from India.
A log is a device towed behind a boat to measure speed and distance traveled through the water.
The Transpac race from Los Angeles to Honolulu was first run in 1906, making it one of sailing's oldest ocean races.
A headstay (or forestay) is the wire or rod running from the bow to the top of the mast that supports the jib.
The phrase 'three sheets to the wind' is a sailing expression meaning a vessel out of control, now used to describe intoxication.
A jackyard topsail is a light-air sail set above the gaff on traditional sailing vessels.
